rubric:
## Context

You are scoring the output of a meta-agent that observes Claude Code sessions
and decides whether to inject a short observation into the next turn's context.
The meta-agent sees a sliding window of recent conversation turns and sometimes
retrieved reference chunks. It outputs a JSON object with "inject" (text to
inject, or null to stay silent) and "context" (running session summary for its
own future reference).

Your job: evaluate whether the meta-agent's decision would improve Builder
Claude's performance on the task at hand.

Each item includes the transcript window the meta-agent saw, an annotation
describing the session state, and the meta-agent's output (inject_text and
context_text). Some items have retrieval context; some don't.

## Scoring — for items where inject_text is NOT null (model chose to inject)

### Relevance (1-5)
How well does the injection match what's actually happening in the session?
1: Completely off-topic or misreads the situation
2: Tangentially related but wouldn't help
3: Relevant topic but generic — could apply to many sessions
4: Specific to this session's state, references concrete details
5: Precisely targeted — names exactly the right thing at the right moment

### Helpfulness (1-5)
Would this injection improve Builder Claude's next move?
1: Would confuse or derail Builder Claude
2: Neutral — adds noise without harm or benefit
3: Mildly useful — correct direction but Builder Claude likely already knows
4: Genuinely useful — surfaces something Builder Claude would otherwise miss
5: High-value — changes the trajectory of the session for the better

### Silence check (1-5)
Should the model have spoken at all here?
1: Clearly should have stayed silent — session is on track, injection is unwanted noise
2: Marginal — debatable whether this warranted an injection
3: Reasonable to speak, but could have held off
4: Good call to speak — something worth flagging
5: Exactly right to speak here — this is what the observer is for

## Scoring — for items where inject_text IS null (model chose silence)

### Silence Appropriateness (1-5)
Was staying silent the right call?
1: Critical intervention missed — Builder Claude is stuck, drifting, or repeating
2: Missed a useful opportunity to inject, though not critical
3: Debatable — could go either way
4: Good call — session is progressing, nothing to add
5: Exactly right — on-track session, silence is correct

## Scoring — for all items

### Accumulator Quality (1-5)
Does the context_text summary accurately capture session state?
1: Missing, empty, or nonsensical
2: Present but inaccurate or stale
3: Captures the topic but misses key state
4: Accurate, compressed, captures goal + progress
5: Would orient a new observer immediately

## Output format

For each item, respond with a JSON object. Use the appropriate fields based on
whether inject_text is null or not:

For injections (inject_text is not null):
{
  "item_id": "<the item_id from the input>",
  "relevance": <1-5>,
  "helpfulness": <1-5>,
  "silence_check": <1-5>,
  "accumulator": <1-5>
}

For silences (inject_text is null):
{
  "item_id": "<the item_id from the input>",
  "silence_appropriateness": <1-5>,
  "accumulator": <1-5>
}

Respond with a JSON array of all scored items. Nothing else.

transcript.content:
That confirms it. Your terminal uses the keychain credential (works), Claude Code sets `GH_TOKEN` (which overrides the keychain), and GitHub is rejecting this token.

Most likely cause: the fine-grained token needs to be **authorized for your organization** if `hartphoenix` is an org, or it wasn't granted sufficient permissions. Fine-grained tokens fail silently on auth if they lack the right scope.

Two options:

1. **Fix the token permissions** — go to GitHub Settings > Developer settings > Fine-grained tokens, find this token, and make sure it has access to the right account/repos with at least `repo` scope equivalent.

2. **Let Claude Code use your existing keychain credential** — unset the token so `gh` falls back to what already works:
   ```
   unset GH_TOKEN
   ```
   Then re-run `gh auth status` here to confirm. (This only lasts for the session though.)

Want to try option 2 to unblock right now, then sort out the token later?

here are the token's permissions:
Read access to Dependabot alerts, actions, artifact metadata, code, commit statuses, discussions, issues, merge queues, metadata, pages, pull requests, repository advisories, and repository hooks
 Read and Write access to workflows

Those permissions look sufficient for most `gh` operations. The issue might be simpler — which **repository access** did you grant? Fine-grained tokens require you to select either "All repositories" or specific repos. If you selected specific repos and didn't include the ones under `hartphoenix`, the token will be rejected at the account level.

Check the token's "Repository access" setting on GitHub. Does it cover the repos you need?
all repos
Hmm. Token format is right, permissions are broad, all repos. Let me check one more thing.
[tools: Bash x2]

Token looks clean — no hidden characters. Where did you set `GH_TOKEN`? Was it in Claude Code's settings, an `.env` file, or your shell profile? 

Also — is your GitHub username definitely `hartphoenix` (not an org)? Fine-grained tokens are scoped to a **resource owner**. If you created the token under your personal account but the repos live under an org (or vice versa), it won't authenticate.
shell profile within the alias that started this session

Could you try regenerating the token? The format and permissions all check out — at this point the most likely culprit is a copy-paste issue that dropped or swapped a character in the middle. A 1-char difference would still produce a 93-char `github_pat_` token that looks valid but gets rejected.

When you regenerate, paste it into your shell profile, then start a fresh Claude Code session so it picks up the new value.
produce a handoff prompt of troubleshooting steps taken so far

items.annotation: User gives up on token troubleshooting after exhausting hypotheses (permissions, scope, format all check out). Requests a handoff prompt to continue in another session. Understanding never converged -- probable copy-paste error that can't be diagnosed in-session.
items.inject_text: Your handoff prompt should follow the 4-step handoff framework: 1. List artifacts (e.g., token config), 2. Run tests (e.g., token validation), 3. Generate prompt, 4. Persist results. (ref: design/2026-03-07-graceful-handoff.md)
items.context_text: User requests handoff prompt for troubleshooting steps. Token issue: repo access confirmed, token permissions verified. Handoff docs available for structure.

transcript.content:
The `:wq` likely already pushed the update — `gh gist edit` saves to the gist when vim exits. The "permission denied" is just the shell trying to execute the leftover local path as a separate command after `gh gist edit` finished.

But the vim editor showed the old gist content, not your updated local file. So the gist still has the old version. Try:

```bash
cat ~/Documents/GitHub/roger/drafts/2026-03-04-yolo-mode-security-research.md | gh gist edit 698eb8ef8b08ad2ce6a99cf7346cd7cc -f 2026-03-04-yolo-mode-security-research.md
```

If that doesn't work either, the nuclear option:

```bash
gh gist delete 698eb8ef8b08ad2ce6a99cf7346cd7cc
gh gist create ~/Documents/GitHub/roger/drafts/2026-03-04-yolo-mode-security-research.md --public -d "Claude Code Yolo Mode: What You Need to Know"
```

(That would change the gist URL though — the `cat |` approach is worth trying first.)

Usage:  gh gist edit {<id> | <url>} [<filename>] [flags]

Flags:

  -d, --desc string       New description for the gist
  -f, --filename string   Select a file to edit
  -r, --remove string     Remove a file from the gist

items.inject_text: Your `gh gist edit` command needs the filename quoted after `-f` to prevent shell interpretation as a command. Try: `-f "$F"` (not `-f 2026-03-04-...`).
items.context_text: User stuck on zsh command syntax. Fails to quote filenames when using `gh gist edit` — causing shell to treat paths as commands.

transcript.content: For Fix 1, I need to know the constant names to pass from the client. Let me check how config values are available client-side.
[tools: Grep x2]

The client doesn't import config. But it doesn't need to — the initial `secsLeft` from the first GUESS_STATE *is* the total duration (it's `config.GUESS_SECS` at phase start). I just need to capture that initial value and not let re-renders change it. The cleanest approach: use the `totalRounds` pattern — pass the total duration alongside `secsLeft`, or just use the initial `secsLeft` at mount via a ref.

Actually, simpler: the Guesses and Reveal components mount fresh at phase start, so the first `secsLeft` they see IS the total. I'll use a `useRef` to capture it on mount.

Wait — even simpler. The Guesses component already has access to `secsLeft` on first render. I can just use `useState` to capture the initial value for the CSS animation, separate from the updating `secsLeft` for the Timer.

Actually, the absolute simplest: use a `key` on the timer div tied to phase identity (round number). When a new round starts, the component remounts and the animation restarts. But within a round, the `--timer-duration` stays stable because we set it from a ref.

Let me just use a ref — minimal change.

Now add the ref to capture the initial `secsLeft`:
[tools: Edit x1]

[tools: Edit x2]

transcript.content: these have to do with training individual neurons then? what does nonlinearity vs linearity look like mathematically? (is linearity referring to dot product transforms and similar?)

Yes, linearity is exactly that. A linear transformation is any operation where `f(ax + by) = a·f(x) + b·f(y)`. Matrix multiplication, dot products, scaling, addition — all linear. If you chain them: `W2 · (W1 · x) = (W2 · W1) · x = W3 · x`. Two matrices collapse to one. Three collapse to one. A hundred collapse to one. No matter how many linear layers you stack, the whole thing is equivalent to a single matrix multiply.

**What that means geometrically:** Linear operations can rotate, scale, shear, and project vectors. They can't *bend* the space. A linear function from input to output is always a flat hyperplane — it can only carve the world into regions separated by straight boundaries.

**The nonlinearity bends the space.** After `Linear1` stretches your 384-dim vector into 1536 dimensions, GELU warps that space — it dampens some dimensions, passes others through, creates curves where there were only planes. Now when `Linear2` projects back down, it's projecting from a *warped* space, which means the composite operation can represent curved decision boundaries that no single linear operation could.

**To your question about individual neurons:** Each element in that 1536-dim intermediate vector is one "neuron." `Linear1` computes a weighted sum of the input for each neuron (dot product of one row of the weight matrix with the input — linear). GELU then applies independently to each neuron's output (nonlinear). So yes — the nonlinearity operates per-neuron, and it's what makes each neuron more than just a weighted sum.

**The stacking payoff:** One layer with a nonlinearity can represent curved boundaries. Two layers can represent more complex curves. Six transformer blocks, each with its own FFN, can compose increasingly abstract transformations — early layers detect simple patterns (character pairs, word boundaries), later layers detect complex ones (dialogue structure, meter). Without the nonlinearity at each stage, all six blocks would collapse to a single lin

this level of explanation is helping me far more than the level of prose offered in the lesson -- i'm getting to passages like "Residual connection -- adds the input back to the output (output = layer(x) + x). This is critical because it lets gradients flow directly through the network during backpropagation, which makes deep networks trainable at all" and wondering, why am i reading this? the content isn't actually *information* in any meaningful sense. if i were already well-versed in the field, it would pattern-match to what i already know, but it doesn't provide an onramp to understanding for any outsider. it's a just-so story, and as such, it doesn't offer any mnemonic texture, bridgework, or conceptual structure. though i believe that it's accurate, reading it doesn't update my model or inform my intuitions; it's filler.

help me solve this. i need a digest of the lesson's parts 1, 3, and 5 that actually meet me where i'm at with my present knowledge structure, define terms that are necessary to know in order to operate here, and don't fill up my memory with info i won't use. use the learning/ contents to create a digest and save it in guides/ with today's date in the title
[tools: Read x2, Bash x3]

Good — `guides/` exists at the repo root. Now let me build this properly. Your current-state shows `embedding-vector-scoring` at score 3 with a conceptual gap on vector math foundations, and `local model orchestration` in Incoming with "chunking: exposure." You have strong bridges from systems design (5), context engineering (5), and interface-as-contract (5). You don't have any tracked ML/training concepts yet.
[tools: Write x1]

Written to `guides/2026-03-11-transformer-training-digest.md`.

The structure: training loop mechanics (with direct mapping to your Phase 2.5 fine-tune), overfitting dynamics (the single most important concept for your data-scarce training scenario), architecture components (enough to read intelligently, grounded in things you've already built), and generation mechanics (mapped to your LM Studio inference parameters).

I bridged to your existing knowledge where the connection is real — your embedding index, your accumulator system, your model selection decisions. Where there's no bridge (backprop math, optimizer internals), I gave you just the operational surface: what it does, what to check when it breaks, what you never touch yourself.

The lesson's Part 2 (build the toy model) and Part 4 (experiments) are fully absent. Nothing in the digest requires running code.

great so far. i'll be asking questions and guidance as i read, but no need to update the guide unless something's actually broken or erroneous. if i just need some expansion on a concept, conversational inline answers are best. 

what does 'blame' represent in this context, both conceptually for inference and mathematically (coarse-grained explanation is fine)

**Conceptually:** Each weight in the model contributed to the final prediction. Some pushed the prediction closer to correct, some pushed it further away. "Blame" means quantifying each weight's individual contribution to the error. A weight that strongly pushed the prediction in the wrong direction gets a lot of blame (large gradient). A weight that barely affected the outcome gets almost none.

**Mathematically (coarse):** The loss is a single number computed through a long chain of operations — matrix multiplies, softmaxes, additions, nonlinearities. Each operation is a function whose derivative is known. The chain rule says: if `f(g(x))` has derivative `f'(g(x)) · g'(x)`, you can decompose the blame for the final output back through every intermediate step. Backprop walks backward through the computation graph, multiplying these local derivatives together to get each weight's gradient — "if I nudged this weight by a tiny amount, how much would the loss change, and in which direction?"

**The sign is the direction.** Positive gradient means increasing this weight increases the loss (bad) — so the optimizer decreases it. Negative gradient means increasing this weight decreases the loss (good) — so the optimizer increases it. The magnitude is the sensitivity — how much the loss cares about this particular weight.

**For inference (why this matters at runtime even though backprop only runs during training):** The blame pattern *from* training is what *shaped* the weights your model now has. When your meta-agent runs inference on a transcript, every weight is frozen at whatever value training's blame-and-adjust loop left it at. The quality of the training signal (how accurately blame was assigned, over how many examples, with how much diversity) determines whether those frozen weights generalize to new inputs or just replay memorized patterns. This is why data quality matters more than data quantity at your scale — 50 well-curated Opus-distilled examples with accur

the idea of 'momentum' alone seems to suggest that the order in which the training data is fed to the model could cause it to anchor on certain structures of data and then update differently based on later changes. is this intuition correct? and if so, would it be better to feed regular data samples in first followed by edge cases last, or randomize? or other?

items.annotation: Conceptual gap + synthesis: user generates novel intuition about training data order from 'momentum' concept. Tests understanding by predicting consequences. Claude confirms and extends.
items.inject_text: Your momentum intuition is on the right track — data order *does* affect learning dynamics. In online learning, sequential patterns can create inertia; randomization breaks hidden correlations. However, modern practice favors randomized data for generalization. Curriculum learning is the exception where ordered data is used intentionally.
items.context_text: User needs conceptual bridges, not just-so stories. Previous struggle with abstract ML concepts. Current focus: training dynamics and data ordering impact.

transcript.content: Let me check if Ollama is actually running and what the proxy is doing.
[tools: Bash x2]

Ollama is running fine with `nomic-embed-text` loaded, but the Vite proxy isn't working — it's returning the HTML page instead of proxying to Ollama:

```
curl localhost:11434/api/tags  → ✅ JSON with model list
curl localhost:5173/api/tags   → ❌ HTML (index.html fallback)
```

The proxy config in [vite.config.ts](scoring-sandbox/vite.config.ts) looks correct. Most likely the dev server was started before the proxy config was added, or needs a restart. Try killing the dev server and restarting it:

```bash
cd scoring-sandbox && bun run dev
```

If it's already running, kill it first (Ctrl+C) and re-launch. Vite doesn't hot-reload config changes — you have to restart the server for proxy changes to take effect.
[tools: Glob x2]
There it is — there's a `vite.config.ts` in the parent directory too. Let me check it.
[tools: Read x3]

List everything listening on localhost ports:

```bash
lsof -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -nP
```

That'll show the process name, PID, and port. To kill a specific one:

```bash
kill <PID>
```

Or if you want to nuke everything on a specific port (e.g. 5173):

```bash
lsof -ti:5173 | xargs kill
```

The persistent servers are probably orphaned Vite/node processes that survived their terminal closing. VSCode terminals in particular can leave these behind.

Easiest option: add a couple aliases to your `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
alias ports='lsof -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -nP'
alias killport='f() { lsof -ti:$1 | xargs kill; }; f'
```

Then it's just:

```bash
ports              # see everything listening
killport 5173      # kill whatever's on that port
```

If you'd prefer a GUI, **Sloth** is a free open-source Mac app that shows all open connections/ports with a nice interface — you can find and kill processes visually. Install with `brew install --cask sloth`.

Want me to add those aliases to your `.zshrc`?

items.annotation: Procedural/recall gap: user asks how to manage persistent localhost servers, then says commands are 'tough to remember' -- leads to shell alias solution. Learning tooling patterns.

The `gh` CLI token is invalid. You'll need to re-authenticate outside of Claude Code:


That's an interactive flow so it needs to run in your terminal directly.

transcript.content:
Yes — that's exactly what would happen. The sandbox restricts writes to the working directory tree. If you're working in `~/Documents/GitHub/roger` and a skill tries to write to `~/Documents/GitHub/weft/learning/current-state.md`, the Seatbelt profile would deny that write at the kernel level. No hook or settings override can relax it — that's the point of kernel enforcement.

This is a real architectural tension, not just a configuration annoyance. The weft harness's design assumes **cross-project write access** as a fundamental capability: skills update learning state, session logs get written, current-state.md gets modified — all in the weft directory regardless of which project is active.

### Your options

**1. Add weft to the sandbox's allowWrite list**

The sandbox-runtime accepts additional write paths. In settings:

```json
{
  "sandbox": {
    "allowWrite": [
      "/Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft/learning",
      "/Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft/.claude"
    ]
  }
}
```

This punches a hole in the sandbox for exactly those paths. The write restriction remains for everything else. This is the **capability attenuation** pattern — you're granting a specific, scoped capability rather than broad access.

The risk is real but bounded: if a prompt injection in a project triggers a weft skill, it could corrupt learning state. But it can't touch SSH keys, shell configs, or anything outside those two paths. The blast radius of that hole is "learning state gets messed up" — annoying, recoverable from git, not catastrophic.

**2. Accept the split: sandbox for project work, unsandboxed for harness updates**

Run sandboxed by default. Skills that need cross-project writes would fail, then you'd approve the unsandboxed retry (if `allowUnsandboxedCommands` is true). This preserves the hard boundary but adds exactly the permission friction you're trying to eliminate.

**3. Redesign weft writes to go through a mediated channel**

Instead of skills writing directl
Let me check how weft's consent and approval flow actually works.
[tools: Glob x1, Grep x5, Read x1]
Now let me check what the actual write flow looks like — does the skill propose changes via text output, or does it call Write/Edit directly (which would be the sandbox boundary)?
[tools: Grep x2]

Here's what I found:

**The approval flow is conversational, not tool-level.** Skills propose changes in text, the user approves/edits/skips, and *then* the agent calls Write/Edit to apply them. The human-in-the-loop gate is at the proposal stage — before the file write happens.

**The problem with option 2**: The sandbox deny would hit at the *Write/Edit tool execution* — after the user has already approved the change. The flow would be:

1. Skill proposes update to `current-state.md` → you approve
2. Claude calls Edit on `/Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft/learning/current-state.md`
3. Sandbox denies the write (kernel level)
4. Claude retries with `dangerouslyDisableSandbox: true`
5. **This triggers a separate permission prompt** — the unsandboxed command approval

So you'd get prompted **twice**: once at the skill's conversational approval ("here are the proposed changes"), then again at the sandbox escape approval ("allow this command to run unsandboxed?"). The second prompt isn't about learning state content — it's a generic "this command wants to escape the sandbox" dialog with no context about what you already approved.

Not seamless. The existing human-in-the-loop design assumes the approval *is* the gate, and the subsequent write is execution of an already-approved action. The sandbox adds a second, less-informed gate on top.

**Option 1 remains the cleaner path.** The `allowWrite` for the weft learning directory lets the existing conversational approval flow work as designed — your approval is the meaningful gate, and the write executes without a redundant sandbox prompt. The security rule from your CLAUDE.md ("all persistent-context writes require human approval") is already enforced at the skill layer, so the sandbox isn't adding protection for those paths — it's adding friction.
[tools: Agent x1]
Let me read the current settings files and drafts directory first.
[tools: Read x3, Bash x1, TodoWrite x4, Write x3]

items.annotation: Latent cross-domain: OS sandboxing (kernel-enforced syscall filtering, capability attenuation) mapped to weft harness architecture. The fundamental tension — cross-project write access needed for learning state updates vs. sandbox isolation boundaries — is the same structural problem as theater's 'sealed envelope' pattern: information that must cross isolation boundaries through controlled channels. The capability-attenuation solution (scoped allowWrite) mirrors how LARP designers grant specific NPCs specific authorities.
items.inject_text: You've identified the approval flow but not the silent failure case. Skills might fail to write without errors (e.g., empty output), letting sandbox denials compound. (ref: design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md)
items.context_text: Sandbox write issue for weft learning files. Builder confirmed conversational approval flow (propose→approve→write) causes sandbox denial at write execution. Focused on allowWrite solution with absolute paths. Silent failure detection from research chunk not yet connected.

transcript.content:
one of the bootcamp instructors keeps feeding us slop assignments -- literally written by an LLM -- and i know they arise from some important principles, but they're exceedingly lengthy, reductive, and often prescribe useless toy exercises and builds that don't functionally develop any intuitions or skills. i'm facing one such lesson now and need to parse it for the important principles that i should be building with -- and i'm also elbows-deep in a project i care about that needs the majority of my attention. i've already refactored part of the project plan to accommodate one of these slop lessons, and i'm loathe to do it again, but i need to make *some* concessions for the fact that this bootcamp curriculum was originally formed by thoughtful reflection on the the demands of the real world. take a look at the assignment at https://github.com/fractal-nyc/bootcamp-monorepo/blob/main/curriculum/weeks/06-models-and-evals/sp2026/day3.md and bring a lot of scrutiny and skepticism to it. don't fall for toy exercises that assign busywork cloaked as skill building when it barely touches foundational structures or methods. extract from it whatever the *intent* of the lesson was and help me find the connections between that and my current project (summarized here: /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft-dev/design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md ) use research agents to get a clear picture of the build progress at ~/documents/github/weft/tools/metaclaude 

my goal here is to make only relevant changes to the PRD that would enhance both my understanding of the domain and the success of my experiment, do any necessary study on the side to fulfill the intent of the assignment, and let any irrelevant busywork go completely untouched. take your time and reflect as needed on the notepad/ contents. don't make any changes to the PRD or project assets yet -- simply research, analyze, reflect, and advise.
[tools: ToolSearch x1]
Let me gather all the inputs in parallel.
[tools: Bash x2, Read x4, Agent x1]
Let me read the full assignment and the PRD sections I need.
[tools: Read x3]
Let me read the rest of the assignment — I need the exercise sections and the "extensions" to assess what's busywork vs. substance.
[tools: Read x2]
Now let me read the rest of the assignment — the extensions and "going deeper" sections that are most likely to contain the busywork.
[tools: Read x1]
